_note: Starter draft. All subs sourced as 'Claude (draft)' until verified against primary sources (NN/g, Google Search Central, web.dev, schema.org). Slugs match the existing scaffolds; the loader merges subs onto them.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Define who the start-page is for first — the largest user segment, not the easiest to design for.
sub_considerations.body: Homepages drift toward the audience that's easiest to please: existing customers who already know what they want. The largest inbound segment is usually less clued-in — first-time visitors arriving from search or referral. Design for them first; the loyal users can find their way.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Beyond 2-3 audiences and the start-page becomes a directory. Three real audiences usually means three landing pages.
sub_considerations.body: Each named audience claims hero real estate, a pathway, and supporting content. Four claims and nothing gets enough room; the page becomes a hub of half-finished promises. Split into purpose-built landing pages and link from a short top-level pathway block.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Treat the start-page as a router. Most users came from a search result — they don't need to land here, they need to leave for the right destination.
sub_considerations.body: Time-on-page is the wrong metric for a homepage. The job is to identify the visitor's intent and forward them in one click. Long dwell time on the start-page usually means the routing failed — they're trying to figure out where to go.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Inbound traffic split shapes the layout. SEO-dominant homepages need topic hubs; campaign-dominant ones need the campaign above everything else.
sub_considerations.body: Pull the actual referrer + landing-page report before redesigning. A homepage that gets 80% of traffic from brand search is doing a different job from one that gets 80% from a paid campaign. Build the layout around the dominant intent, not a hypothetical average user.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Top-level nav reflects the user's mental model, not the org chart. "Services" and "Solutions" as siblings is internal-speak.
sub_considerations.body: Internal labels leak into navigation when teams own their own menu item. Audit the labels with users (a five-second test will do): can they predict what's behind each one? If not, the label is named after a department, not a destination.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Cap primary nav at 5–7 items. The 8th belongs in a submenu, footer, or a different page.
sub_considerations.body: Above seven items the bar becomes a list — users stop scanning and start reading. If your IA needs more, group siblings under a parent label and use a flyout. Don't pad the bar to fill it; whitespace beats a parked menu item.

sub_considerations.one_liner: The hero answers "what is this and who is it for" in 6 words or less. Brand poetry comes after.
sub_considerations.body: First-time visitors need to confirm they're in the right place before they'll read further. A short, plain headline ("Project management for design teams") beats a clever one ("Where great teams ship") for that single test. Save tone for the supporting line below.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Image-heavy heroes delay LCP. Preload the hero asset, set explicit dimensions, and never loading="lazy" it.
sub_considerations.body: The hero image is almost always the LCP element on a start-page. <link rel="preload" as="image" fetchpriority="high"> shaves real time off the metric; width and height attributes prevent CLS while it loads; loading="lazy" on the hero is a classic own-goal.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Two clear pathways beat five soft ones. Choice paralysis on a start-page is a measurable conversion leak.
sub_considerations.body: Every extra pathway dilutes the others' click-rate. Pick the two jobs that account for most of your real-world traffic value and make them obvious; let exploration handle the long tail. The temptation to give every team a pathway is the homepage's death-by-committee.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Rank pathway placement by traffic value, not internal politics. The most common job gets the visual weight.
sub_considerations.body: The pathway block tends to mirror the org chart over time. Reset the order by actual session value: top-left or hero-CTA goes to the biggest contributor, not the noisiest stakeholder. Document the rationale so the next redesign doesn't quietly revert it.

sub_considerations.one_liner: One next action above the fold; the rest is exploration. The fold isn't dead, it's just louder.
sub_considerations.body: "Above the fold" still means the first viewport the user sees, and it's still the most-attended pixel on the page. Spend it on one clear next step — the headline, a supporting line, the primary CTA. Cramming secondary blocks up there robs the primary of its weight.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Cards in a row imply equal weight. If one matters more, give it a different size, colour, or treatment.
sub_considerations.body: Four identical cards say "these are interchangeable." If one is the primary path and three are alternatives, design has to break the symmetry — a feature card, a tinted background, a bigger image. Otherwise the user picks by position, not intent.

sub_considerations.one_liner: "Latest" fades the moment you stop publishing. Curate manually, or auto-with-fallback.
sub_considerations.body: An auto-populated "Latest articles" block on a start-page is a maintenance smell: a quiet month makes the homepage look abandoned. Either commit to a publishing cadence, or curate the block manually with evergreen picks and refresh it monthly.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Featured content needs an editorial reason — surface what's good, not what's new.
sub_considerations.body: "Newest" is a cheap heuristic that gives recency points to weak content and buries strong evergreen pieces. Promote items because they answer a common visitor question or showcase the work you want to be known for, not because they were published last Tuesday.

sub_considerations.one_liner: One primary CTA per visual zone. Two competing primaries cancel each other.
sub_considerations.body: If the hero has two equal-weight buttons, neither reads as primary — the user defaults to the left, or skips both. Pick the action you most want; demote the other to a secondary button (outlined, ghost) or a text link. Equal-weight pairs are an anti-pattern.

sub_considerations.one_liner: CTA copy describes the destination, not the action. "Browse plans" beats "Click here" or "Submit".
sub_considerations.body: The user predicts what'll happen on click; the button label is the prediction. Verbs matched to destination nouns ("See pricing", "Read the case study", "Start a free trial") set accurate expectations. Generic verbs ("Learn more", "Continue") leak users by leaving the destination ambiguous.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Footer is the fallback nav, not a sitemap dump. Group by user intent: company, support, legal, social.
sub_considerations.body: A footer with 60 unstructured links is a sitemap that nobody reads. Three or four labelled columns let users predict where to look — and let you cut links that don't earn their place. "Site map" as a single link below the columns covers the long tail honestly.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Repeat the most-used primary links in the footer. Mobile users scroll past the header to find them.
sub_considerations.body: On long mobile pages, hauling the thumb back up to a sticky header is friction; the footer is closer. The top 3-5 primary nav items belong in both places. Duplication isn't a sin when reading order matters.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Don't ask for an email above the fold. The user hasn't decided you're worth it yet.
sub_considerations.body: Asking for an email before the user has read anything is the digital equivalent of being approached at the door. Place the newsletter prompt after at least one body section — once the visitor has signalled they're interested by scrolling.

sub_considerations.one_liner: "Updates" is not a value proposition. Say what you send and how often.
sub_considerations.body: "Sign up for updates" gives the user no way to predict the relationship. "Once a month, a 5-minute read on shipping AI products" sets expectations both parties can meet. The opt-in rate goes up when the implicit contract is explicit.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Hero LCP target: under 2.5s on a slow 4G. The hero image and the font determine this.
sub_considerations.body: The 75th-percentile LCP threshold for "Good" is 2.5s. On a start-page that almost always means optimising one image and one font: a properly-sized hero asset (AVIF or WebP, fetchpriority="high"), and a font with font-display: swap + preload of the woff2 file used in the hero text.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Homepage HTML payload cap: ~50–80KB compressed. The start-page is the budget canary — if it bloats, the rest will too.
sub_considerations.body: The start-page tends to accumulate components faster than any other page (marketing experiments, partner logos, testimonial sliders). Set an explicit byte budget for the compressed HTML and reject changes that push past it without removing something else. The discipline propagates downstream.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Add Schema.org WebSite + SearchAction so Google can render a sitelinks search box on brand SERPs.
sub_considerations.body: The sitelinks search box appears under your brand SERP listing when Google detects WebSite schema with a SearchAction pointing at your site search. It dramatically lifts brand-search engagement — and you can't get it any other way. Put the JSON-LD in the homepage <head>.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Confirm the brand name with Organization schema. Pick the name users actually say, not the legal entity.
sub_considerations.body: The name field in Organization JSON-LD is the canonical brand label Google will display. If your legal name is "Acme Holdings, Inc." but everyone calls you "Acme", set name: "Acme" and put the legal name in legalName. Mismatches here surface as awkward SERP labels.
